name: Farro or Spelt (Weir)
recipeInstructions:
Recipe from "Going With an Ancient Grain: Resilient, nutty farro adds crunch to autumn vegetable soup," by Joanne Weir for San Francisco Chronicle 11/26/97. A travel story set in Rapolano, a village halfway between Siena and Arezzo in Tuscany.
1. Make big, steamy bowls of autumn vegetable soup with farro (an ancient wheat grain shaped like rice and colored reddish-brown) and cannellini beans.
2. Drizzle the soup with her own golden-green olive oil.
3. Farro is an ancient type of wheat known as Triticum dicoccum, cultivated by the Romans who turned it into a thick soup called puls thousands of years ago.
4. Today, Tuscans around Lucca and the surrounding hills continue to cook the grain daily.
5. Farro resembles rice with brown hulls attached; it cooks up like barley.
6. This resilient grain is not prone to disease, so no pesticides are needed in its production.
7. Look for farro or spelt at your local health food store in the bins of grains.
